What's going on everybody? Hellooo! Dr. Cascio here and I wanted to discuss a very important topic of adverse effects. I'm going to list a few things out here for you. Okay, nausea, vomiting, heartburn, inflammation, intestinal blockage, stomach cramps, fatigue, headache, sleepiness, insomnia, diarrhea, insomnia, peristhesia. All these things sound familiar, right? For vitamin C. I just wanted to keep that in mind when describing these sorts of effects and I wanted to describe the impact things like no SIBO may have when somebody may take a dietary supplement of any kind and long story short that basically turns into if somebody takes something and thinks they have some sort of negative effect and then reports it to somebody else, they're also going to be like, hmm yeah, I may have also felt a little itchy scratch mo here as well and before you know it, there's like a fake case of conjunctive itis going around the entire gym. So if any of these things are reported, make sure to look at it objectively, especially when discussing it in the group because it may actually prompt you to go asking people about it and then they're going to try and identify with the question that you're asking which may actually fuel the fire. So I wanted to let you know that extra care was taken to make sure that these formulae plus one for vocab do not interact to my knowledge with like vast amounts of prescriptions available. I took extra care for that now. Nobody can ever know exactly for sure there aren't a stack of clinical trials for every single freaking dietary supplement ingredient with every single prescription drug known to man. That's just completely unreasonable. But I take the position that since these things are generally recognized as safe by the Food and Drug Administration and that they are found in foods to some degree that the chances of interacting with most prescription medications is slim to none and that many side effect profiles are the result of no SIBO which is the opposite of placebo and the possible interaction with a person that has a sensitive stomach. So for most people that have known sensitive stomachs it is recommended that they always take this with food and when I say with food I think it's within like an hour of eating. So like you can eat and then take a pill an hour later and I would still consider that in my opinion with food. But I would always if people have sensitive stomachs always make sure to take it with food and then nothing that was formulated I don't think needs to be taken with food per se to make sure that people don't have issues. But just know where this stands always make sure to look at these look at these um I don't want to call it these reports objectively and always make sure to take other aspects of this person's lifestyle into account because people are on other supplements and other prescriptions they may not be telling you about they may be like oh my goodness the intra workout it made me so sleepy but it just so happens like the last week they slept four hours per night and it just so happens on day seven they decided they were gonna feel super crappy about it and then blame the intra. So there are things like this that need to be considered I strongly advise you to check out that list of reported side effects of vitamin C ingestion and take a lot of these things in stride because these formulae are made with ingredients that are generally recognized as safe they are formulated with care to make sure that they do not interact with a vast array of prescription drugs and I am confident in saying these things are safe so I hope you found that valuable.
